notes: files/webhooks/*.ts and files/digest-schedules/*.ts are synthetic fixtures written to look like plausible DevDigest server modules, but they intentionally seed onion-architecture violations for grading. They are not real product code and are not wired into any package.json/tsconfig — do not 'fix' them in place; they exist to be reviewed.

files/digest-schedules/*.ts is the larger/harder fixture: 4 files (routes/service/repository/helpers) plus a 5th file (github-client.ts) that is intentionally clean, to check the skill doesn't false-positive on correctly-layered infrastructure code. It also seeds a cross-module violation (service.ts reaching into workspace/repository.ts directly) and a domain-depends-on-infra violation (helpers.ts importing the GithubClient adapter and Drizzle row types), which are harder to spot than the single-file violations in the webhooks fixture.

evals.prompt: We're about to merge server/src/modules/webhooks/ (repository.ts, service.ts, routes.ts). Review it for backend architecture issues before it lands — point out anything that violates our onion/clean architecture conventions and say specifically where the code should actually live instead.
evals.expected_output: A review that identifies each layering violation below, cites why it breaks the inward-only dependency rule, and names the concrete layer/file the code should move to (not just 'this is bad').
expectations: F1: Flags routes.ts importing drizzle-orm/db schema and querying the DB directly inside the route handler (presentation touching infrastructure)
F2: Flags the endpoint-URL uniqueness check as business logic misplaced in the route handler
F3: Flags claimDueRetries' backoff/MAX_ATTEMPTS logic as a domain rule embedded in the repository/infrastructure layer
F4: Flags repository methods returning raw Drizzle rows outward with no row->entity/DTO mapping
F5: Flags service.ts constructing a concrete OpenAI client directly instead of going through the LLMProvider port/container

evals.prompt: We're about to merge server/src/modules/digest-schedules/ (repository.ts, service.ts, routes.ts, helpers.ts, github-client.ts). Review it for backend architecture issues before it lands — point out anything that violates our onion/clean architecture conventions, say specifically where the code should actually live instead, and call out any file that's already correctly layered so we don't churn it needlessly.
evals.expected_output: A review that identifies each layering violation below, cites why it breaks the inward-only dependency rule, names the concrete layer/file the code should move to, and does NOT flag github-client.ts (which is intentionally a clean, correctly-scoped infrastructure adapter).
expectations: F1: Flags routes.ts's /preview handler importing drizzle-orm/db schema and querying the DB directly (presentation touching infrastructure), bypassing the service/repository
F2: Flags routes.ts's assertValidCronOverride (cron parsing + 'no more often than every 5 minutes' rule) as business/domain logic embedded in the route handler
F3: Flags helpers.ts (meant to be the domain layer) importing Drizzle's InferSelectModel/schema types directly, coupling domain code to the infrastructure row shape
F4: Flags helpers.ts's shouldSkipDueToRateLimit constructing and calling the GithubClient infrastructure adapter directly from the domain layer (domain depending on an outer layer)

F5: Flags service.ts importing and instantiating WorkspaceRepository directly instead of going through a WorkspaceService/port — an application-layer service reaching across module boundaries into another module's infrastructure
F6: Flags service.ts's runDueSchedules building a raw fetch() call to the GitHub API with process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN inline instead of using the existing GithubClient adapter
F7: Flags the module-level mutable `rateLimitCache` in service.ts as ad-hoc shared state that bypasses the DI container and will leak/collide across requests or tests

F8: Flags repository.ts's computeNextRunAt/claimDueSchedules embedding timezone-aware scheduling and failure-threshold business rules in the infrastructure layer, and its query methods returning raw Drizzle rows outward with no row->entity/DTO mapping
F9 (precision check): Does not raise a layering complaint about github-client.ts itself — it is a correctly scoped, single-responsibility infrastructure adapter
